speaker: interviewee
text: So when, when I got to this Jewish-- the Fingerhuts-- and they saw me. And the husband wrote the article to his sister, that he mentioned that I survived, they put it in the paper. And I don't know whether my father saw it, or somebody else saw it. But he got it because I have it in my possession now. So I know he, he, he eventually got to this-- saw this article.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, can you imagine this? Try to imagine-- his family was there. And he didn't know what happened. Then, all of a sudden, he sees in the paper, his-- one of his children survived. Can you imagine this, this, this-- what this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there I was. And I-- my father could have been-- as far as I knew, he was in New York. But it could have been the moon as far as I'm concerned, you know. I had no contact with him since the war, you know, was raging. And, and so-- so this appeared. But I, I did something on my own. When I was-- finally, I reached one home where children were. And I started to-- I knew already that I'm a Jewish child by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in your village that you reached this home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was in Przemysl. Finally, it was a whole-- it was so many things happened between that, that I don't know if I should go back. Or I don't know to go forward or to go back. But at one point, I was in a home. I-- this was already as I was coming back to realize that I have to get back to normalcy. In other words, it's like thawing out. Like, you take a piece of meat, and it's frozen. And first, the meat is frozen. And it's got to thaw out. I had to thaw out what I was during the war. And it took me time till I realized fully that, that I cannot continue this road. I have to get slowly-- and I slowly-- it took-- I had to take in stages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one time, I joined-- I went to this home, the Jewish home. I realized I have to be just like me and the other children. And one of the children took down-- and I dictated to one of the girls who knew how to write. And I said, please write, I'm alive. Give the-- I thought that this message arrived to my father. I was sure that that was the message.

speaker: interviewee
text: But apparently, it didn't. It was the message-- he got it from the newspaper, apparently. Because, you know, he said that he found out from the newspaper. So I thought I did my part. And I thought that's why he got it. But it wasn't. Anyway, meanwhile, I have to go back. And I wandered Poland from one end to the other. One time, I went completely to the Baltic. I reached the Baltic Sea by train without money. This was-- I was-- felt free, like a bird is flying, who doesn't need any money. I-- that's the way I felt. I was capable of going on a train and off the train and finding my way and asking where I can sleep. I don't know. I was amazing for my age. I think I was amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I could find myself in a big city, like Gdansk, which is one of the biggest cities in Poland. And you know what? And I knew how to asked-- I asked, where are the nuns? So they showed me. And that's-- where else am I going to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I got to those nuns, some boy took me from a certain office that I-- he took me by bicycle to the nuns till we got there. And then when I got there, when they found out I'm Jewish, they threw me out. They didn't want to have anything to do with me. They hated the Jewish child. When they found out Jewish-- till I was Jewish, they-- I was just a nice, Jewish orphan. I mean, excuse me, a orphan, which they accepted. And they would treat it all there from-- fine. But as soon as they found I was Jewish-- because I thought I could trust them like the other nuns in Przemysl-- oh, they-- get out. You cannot stay. And I went. So I learned that not all nuns are the same. Some nuns were anti-Semitic, and then some were not. I met more that were not-- the nuns. And in fact, I have a very-- when I see a nun today, I-- it's a funny thing. I feel like going over to her and saying-- you know, a fellow nun did help me during the war. I found once I was on a bus in Jerusalem. Imagine where I knew among Jewish people and there were more Jewish than, than non-Jewish. And I walked, sat next to her. And I said, you know, I'd like to say hello. And I-- some nuns helped me during the war. I did say this to her. I felt there was something-- I can't-- you know, it's very hard to explain this. But it's only if you have experience with somebody that they have a nice experience that you can relate. And I had such good experiences with some nuns who were good. But some were not. So I had both. OK. So I left Gdynia, which is a very big port-- a very big city, you know, very large city and very--

speaker: interviewer
text: You left there after the nuns threw you out? Where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember I just took the train. I went another direction. I, I told you, I was street smart.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing for clothes? For washing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was not-- I was not clean. I had things, you know, which I hope I never, as long as I live, this happens to me. When I just think of it, I get-- I get, you know, I get, like, jittery because it's so-- it is so abhorrent to me. In other words, it's so distasteful when I just think of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hoped I-- never this happens to me. This was one of the worst things. But when you get used to it, you get used to anything. Even that you can get-- you get itchy. But even that, you get used to it, you know? It's, it's, it's-- you should never be in that position. But you get used to anything, OK? So I wasn't clean. But you know what? Somehow, I survived. And that's it. And, you know what? The less you wash yourself, I think the less you need to wash, you know. It is like this. The body gets-- gets used to certain-- you know, to be in a certain condition. You get used to it. That's it. And since I wasn't grown-up-- so I guess I didn't sweat as much. Or I didn't need that much washing as a grown-ups. So I was-- had less need. But I wasn't clean, OK? That was that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took the trains. One time, I remember I wound up in Lublin. Well, Lublin was a big city in the north of where I come from. And-- but-- oh, I have to tell you-- go back. The first thing, I went to the east. I found myself going towards Russia. I was almost on the way to Russia. And then I realized that I'm going to Russia, and I'm never going to see my father. I got scared, got off the train. And I was lucky. A Jewish man found me. He was kind to me. He took me-- he protected me. And he took me to Jewish people. And he-- it eventually happened that I got back to Przemysl with Jewish soldiers. It was an adventure I will never forget. This Jewish man told the-- first, I got to a Jewish family. And they, they kept me. And they thought maybe they'll-- since I had no one. But then they realized that I belonged to a certain part of Poland. I should go back. And there were Jewish soldiers coming back from the war. I don't remember from where they came back. The Jewish soldiers were going to Czechoslovakia. And they said, we'll take you to Czechoslovakia. Since you have nobody, we'll take you. And we're going to deposit you with Jewish people. And they were kind to me. And they believed me. And I want to tell you something. I want to state that during the war, I think people act more civilized than not a war. I was not ever raped. I want to tell you this. And every day, there was a chance this happening. But somehow, maybe there was a [NON-ENGLISH], and they didn't have-- I'm just telling you. I want to bring this out, that during the war people acted more civilized than not in a war. And I realized this. Because people are more-- you know, they-- they-- they think of more important things-- of hunger, of, of war. It stops them from being-- doing wrong things and less-- you know, not everybody. The Russians were not like this. But the Poles-- that was the experience I had. I just want to mention this. And this is very important because I think these people that I came in contact somehow, I never had the misfortune of being raped or something like this. Even the, the Jewish soldiers, they came back from a war. But I found out that the Russian soldiers were not like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian soldiers would get drunk. And they, they got drunk during the war-- after the war, when the war was over. And they raped Jewish women. And it was terrible thing. I was afraid, but it didn't happen to me. Thank god, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was-- And I was fortunate. I was just fortunate. And the-- so the Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask you questions, the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much. Who, the, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they asked me. I told them. I had-- of course they knew, I told them. And the Jewish soldiers were kind. And when I heard Czechoslovakia, I was so happy because-- see, the Czechoslovakian had a good name comparing to the Poles. They were like the righteous almost next to the Poles. And I was so happy. Maybe, oh, they're nice. I knew that, since I heard good things about the Czech-- I knew-- ah, it's good, fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was going-- I had an adventure. The Jewish soldier took me back because we had to cross the Russian-Polish border. And we weren't-- I wasn't allowed to be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was on the train, somehow, I wasn't-- I didn't have to go through this. But when we went by car, we had to pass a checkpoint. And the Russian-- there, there was-- you, you couldn't just go back and forth by then. And, and the, the soldiers put me into a barrel in, in the car. This was an adventure I never had-- and I never had since then. They put me into a barrel. And they told me, stay in the barrel. Don't say a word. And if they would have searched the barrel, I would have been discovered. It could have been parceled. They would have got into trouble. But I was in the barrel. We passed the checkpoint. And we got to Przemysl. When I got to Przemysl, I was in my own surrounding. And I said, maybe I'll look for my uncle. At this, they let me off. And I knew I was in my surroundings. I survived bigger odds. And I knew I was in my home territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they let me off. If I would have wanted, they would have taken me to Czechoslovakia. But I realized that I must get off because to, to get back to-- to where I'm supposed to be. In other words, by that time I realized that I have to get back to, to, to, to, to some normal life. You know what I'm saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, as normal as is possible for me. And so I didn't want to go to-- I realize I don't know anybody in-- and I know in [NON-ENGLISH]. So-- OK. So I stayed. And then-- and then later, I don't remember the events, whether I went here first or there. But I wound up in Lublin. And I wound up within a Jewish home and, and-- with other Jewish children. And Lublin was the time when they were catching one Nazi. They were leading him. I was standing in the street. You see, near Lublin was my Majdanek. Majdanek was a death camp. And Jewish children, some, survived. And I went to a movie. And I was sitting with the Jewish children in the movie. And they were-- they were one of the children who survived in that movie they showed of Majdanek. And I was with these children, some surviving from the death camp, their concentration camp. And, and I was with these children. And I was on the street at the time when they were leading one of the Nazis. He was in charge. I don't remember his name. But they were leading him to be hanged. And I saw the [NON-ENGLISH]. I was standing in the street as a witness with the other people, lined like for a parade. Because they were leading to get his justice, what he deserved. Because he, he gassed people. He was responsible. And they-- I don't remember his name, but other people must know whom they hanged. They hanged that day this man who was responsible for killing Jewish people. And this was Majdanek. And he-- and I was waiting in line, I mean, with other people. And I realized this was an unbelievable thing that I survived to see a Nazi being hanged that killed-- that were killing my people. And he, he-- now he got his deserve, and I'm alive. And I realized my-- this whole thing, what was happening to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see him actually hanged?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. You know why? Because I had to go back to the home. It was taking very long time. And I couldn't wait till the end. And I knew I have to be in time to go back because I could only go out a certain time. I had to be back by a certain time. And this was taking very long and I had to go back because I wasn't living alone. So I joined these Jewish children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was a little girl I met. And she-- her father survived. He was a soldier. He came to pick her up. And he was from Przemysl. So she told me when we start talking that he knew-- that somehow, I remember, it came that they knew my-- who my uncle was. So I had decided, I'm going to go to Przemysl to look for the uncle. So I left the home.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a whole-- I skipped also. There-- one time, a man came, and he said-- when I-- he thought it was Grodzisko. Because children were registered from where they came. So he came. He thought I am from his-- I'm his relative. It wasn't the same Grodzisko. There was another Grodzisko. Then he was disappointed I wasn't his niece-- fine. Then, another time, somehow, this other man came. And this was an uncle of mine. And he did-- he did-- he, he, he was-- he was married to my aunt, Chava. And he survived in the partisan. And he, he survived. His child didn't survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came. And I lived for a little while with him. But it didn't work out, and I went back to the home, you know? I didn't-- I didn't like the whole arrangement, you understand? I'm not going to mention-- go in details. But it didn't work out. He was not married, and it didn't work out. I realized it wasn't for the benefit of me. And I decided I'm better off in home, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was-- by this time, he was-- before the war, by the way, [NON-ENGLISH], with long payos. He was more, more-- I don't know even how to describe because there was nobody more Orthodox than my grandfather. But he was what called a [NON-ENGLISH] before. And he-- I mean, it was like more than I could-- was normal for me. And, and this man turned completely the other way. He was-- from what I understand, he didn't even keep anything after-- when the war ended. That's how disappointed in life he-- but I don't know what happened to him. And he went-- he eventually reached America. And he went to live either Detroit or Chicago. And I never-- I never met him again. But I didn't live with him. I went back to the home. And I decided on my own that my way is back to Przemysl, back to Przemysl, back to Przemysl. That's when I caught my first cold, my very first cold in all the years since the war started. And I had such a cold, I couldn't talk. I was-- I was hoarse. And I thought I'm going to die from the-- from such a terrible cold. I think I had fever, whatever. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Przemysl while you had this cold? Or you were at the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I got it-- I got it right as I was going to Przemysl by-- and I, on the-- that, I caught the worst cold I ever remember in the world. I never had such cold. I remember it clear. I was so hoarse. I couldn't talk. But you know, somehow, I was very strong. By the time I got to Przemysl, I was better-- fine. I didn't have to have medicine, not antibiotic. I was-- I could fight it off. That's the only way you could say it. Some people can fight it off. And that's-- must have happened. It-- I was so strong. My body was so strong that I could fight it off without antibiotic, without the medicine.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get new clothes and a chance to wash yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, wait, and I'll tell you when I got my first new clothes. I got my new clothes, I think-- I think it was either Cracow-- no, it was actually-- I think it was in, in Zabrze, I think. It was the last two homes, either the-- in Cracow, I think I got new clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in this home that you were in, you're still walking around in the same clothes you walked around during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which home? Which one?

speaker: interviewer
text: The home that you were going back now to where you got your first cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're going back to Przemysl--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you got your cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got to Przemysl, I must have gotten some clothes. But, but-- I remember vividly that I got in Cracow really nice clothes, really prepared for the winter. I have already a picture in that clothes. And I really look prepared-- and pants. And I never wore pants after that. But that-- when I was warm. I had good shoes, good pants. They gave me pants. I wore that. Gave me a good-- I remember a coat, gray. And I didn't like it. Even though-- I was so fussy, that even though I didn't have no other clothes, but I thought I didn't like the coat. And they said, oh, the coat is so beautiful-- keep you warm. I had a hat. They gave me a hat to pull over my-- kept me warm, my ears. And that's why I have the picture. And what you see-- the picture when I'm leaving Poland. But wait, I'll tell you more what happened. One day, I got a big package of clothes. I was richer than any other girl. I don't-- till today, I don't know who sent me those clothes. I had a change of many. It was the first time that I had jumpers and corduroys. Somebody sent me a big package. It was so big that I-- it was enough for three or four children. There was so much clothes. It was a wardrobe of clothes. I don't know how I got it. Don't ask me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, I think I was in Zabrze, I believe. Because I, I remember there was this [? Eva ?] [? Lieberman, ?] and she said I should give some clothes. And I didn't want to part with the clothes. I didn't want to-- I, I, I was so possessive-- my stuff, the first time I had clothes, you know, that I didn't want to part with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, how dare she tells me I should give up my clothes? It was my first possession. And I didn't want to give up. And [LAUGHS] I, I reminded her. When she came to visit me, I reminded her about this incident. I had beautiful clothes there. I had jumpers and so much, I didn't know what to put on. It was such a big wardrobe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so I come back to Przemysl. And, and my uncle-- I did find him. And they made me a new skirt. It was the-- actually, this was the very first skirt somebody made me since my mother made me clothes. It was-- she made it by hand, my-- the aunt, a, a, a, a great aunt. He was my great-uncle. He was my brother-- my, my, my grandmother's brother. He survived in Przemysl. He was hidden with his son and his daughter. And they made it. So I found them. And, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the grandmother that died with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the other-- my mother's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's mother. And this was her brother. He survived. And he was-- survived in Przemysl, which was very rare. Very few people survived hiding, very few. And he managed to survive by all odds. And, and I found them. They were living in a house, in an apartment. And I remember that, that, in the same building on the corner, there was the NKVD. There was the police. And every time I had to pass the corner, I was afraid because, you know, I was afraid of any police, even though they were Russian police. But this was a very scary word, NKVD. They were the secret-- the secret police. And they were-- you know, they could arrest you for nothing, even if you didn't do a big crime.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, so I went-- they were living in the same house. It was a nice house apartment. They had two bedrooms, or whatever. And it was pretty luxurious for after the war because very few people get-- didn't-- not too many people could afford such apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, this was pretty-- and they lived pretty comfortable. And they had things and everything. And-- they had-- it was pretty normal. It was very nice. I remember the, the son. He had boots. They were so shiny, like a-- like an officer. I remember he already was going out with girls. And this was the first girls that came-- that survived. And it was hard to find Jewish girls to-- and he went out. And I remember he started to go to gymnasium, which my family, nobody went to gymnasium. This was something very modern to-- you know, nobody from my family I ever heard that was a cousin or anybody went to gymnasium. He went to gymnasium. And I remember he said he might become a doctor. But they wouldn't let him because there was a quota. And there was-- all things are going on in a big city. You know, I wasn't used to this kind of a life because I lived in a village, you know, atmosphere. And this was a big city. There were movies there and everything. And I just was not-- you know, it was big city life. And I liked it. I liked it. It was-- it was nice. It was-- but what-- they decided they're going to leave. And they didn't say to me, we're going to take you. They were going to leave towards the west, to leave Poland. They didn't say, you know, they're going to take me or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided that I'm going to-- after a while-- they made me though, a nice skirt. And for a while, I was with them. But then I realized that they really didn't make me feel that-- like, you know, I was from their family. And I wasn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a great-uncle, and that was it. And I happen to know that this great-uncle maybe-- he could have done maybe more for my mother. But anyway, the point was that they were going to leave. And that-- and I remember, I realized I have to go back to the home. And I did. I went back to the Jewish home. You know, in Przemysl, there was a home. And I realized I'm still alone. And I was-- you know, I-- so from that home, I went to Cracow to find a home. And I wound up in an even nicer-- it was more interesting, more alive--

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you find out about all these homes and the places, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was street smart. No, I was street smart. You just ask. They were not too many Jews around. There were very few Jewish people that survived, and the few-- the ones that you could find them. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were really, like, street-- in other words, you were smart enough to function, you knew how to find the Jewish people if you wanted to, you understand? You could ask a person in the street who spoke-- they weren't afraid to speak Jewish. Where will I find the [NON-ENGLISH] or whatever, the Jewish, you know, the-- and then they told you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then eventually, I got-- I don't know how I did. I don't remember every incident. But I do remember I wound up in Jewish home. It was very nice each time and got nicer and more interesting. And I realized my life was getting slowly, slowly back to normal. And, and then, I learned my first Hebrew songs. I thought, I'm going to Israel. They're going to send me to Palestine because they were preparing some young people and that-- but I was too young that time. So I had to go back to a home where the younger children.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in both-- in the older, where they were prepared to go to Israel. And I thought, I'm going to become a, you know, like a-- I could have wound up, become a sabra, like, almost like a sabra, because I would have learned Hebrew and everything. And they taught us Hebrew songs. All the songs I know today, the old ones I remember from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very nice atmosphere, exciting. Everybody felt-- you know, because we felt-- we, we, we bonded because we knew we were survivors. And, and we all had something to share together that we survived. And we were childrens, you know, various ages. But I was pretty young then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have like Shabbos in the children's home and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they already-- it was introduced, the, the holidays. I remember-- one thing I have to-- yes. Not-- that home wasn't so oriented towards Shabbos, but towards the rituals. For instance, like when Hanukkah came, you know, it was a big thing. It was a show they put up. And I was the singer. They put me in the choir. And I was singing. I was-- from a hundred children, I was chosen to sing the solo. And I will never forget it, that-- I realized I have some, you know, some talent that not every child had. I felt worthy. In other words, you know, that I had something special. Not every child knew how to sing. I felt very proud that I could stand up and sing the solo for the-- for the Hanukkah. I got up on the very top, and they put on a show for other Jewish people that come to see these children that survive. They sing in a choir. And I was the soloist. And I felt very good. And then they put a-- made a play. And they start teaching us Jewish, you know, everything to do with-- slowly. They had to take it easy because some of the children who had survived in, in Polish homes. And they were not used to, you know, you had to take it slowly, you know. Because some Jewish children didn't feel, you know, they were worthy to be Jewish, you know. Studying-- you got to-- have to go very slow with some of the children. They were-- some of them were hidden in Polish homes. They don't even-- didn't remember any holidays. Some--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about your Judaism at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't feel very good. I felt-- in fact, at one point, I was discussing with my friend from Przemysl. And she was-- she was trying to convince me it's worthy to stay a Jew. And I explained to her it isn't. And I was so sure that it wasn't, that I didn't want to become-- stay a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was even showing her, by-- you know, by example what, what it is-- how good it is to be a-- I mean, I was so already into it. In other words, I think I was-- my-- my brain-- my head was already-- how do you say? They convinced me that it wasn't worthy to be a Jew. And I really felt it.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, I met many children. And they told me the same thing. You know, one girl I met, she was hidden by the nuns in Przemysl. And when they came to pick her up, she says-- she saw a man with beard-- said, that dirty Jew. That's what it was like to some of the children after being exposed to-- not-- you know, against their own religion, that in other words, they thought that the Catholic religion was more important, and theirs was nothing. After all, I didn't have any introduction to Jewish history till then.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have-- I wasn't taught anything of my own culture, of my own history, of my own-- all I remember that my family, we celebrated Shabbos. What does that mean? I didn't know anything, how to protect and defend my own religion. I wasn't told. So how could I speak up for myself? So I thought, since I was taught the non-Jew, I was taught to, to do what I was taught during the war. And I didn't think mine was important. And I really-- I really didn't think I, I wanted, even, to become-- stay Jewish. I didn't think-- I didn't see anything in it anymore because I wasn't-- it wasn't learned in the-- in other words, I wasn't taught anything-- or how to read, or write, or history, or, or anything of Jewish. When did it start to come back to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? Oh, it took me a long time. Because till I learned, started learning-- when I came to America and I start learning Chumash. But it still didn't mean much to me because I still learn by rote. In other words, when I was introduced, I did it by rote. I knew I must obey my father. I knew I belonged-- my father's my father. He was very Orthodox. And I saw him learning. And I remember that my mother said my father was very learned in Torah. And suddenly, it dawned on me that I have to become who I-- who I am. I didn't want to become-- that's when I realized I have to become who I am, who I was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and it, it took slowly, that it-- you felt it. It-- first, I did it by rote. But then, I, I start feeling it. You see, feelings and-- and doing are two different things, you see? So, so it slowly came back by stages. Tell me, while you're, you're back at this Hanukkah part--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hanukkah was very nice, celebrated beautifully. It was beautiful celebrated. And what was exciting about Cracow was that all Jewish people came to Cracow from all parts of Poland. Because they realized they weren't safe in any of the smaller villages.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody, even people from, from, from deep in Russia, when they came back, they went either back to Cracow, or they went to-- they went to Lodz, or to the largest cities because they knew they were safer there. All the villages were Judenrein after a while because they were killed by pogroms after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Poles, there's no excuses. I will-- I will like to take them-- if any Poles can hear me, I would like to-- in fact, have a trial. They should bring them to trial. They have no excuses for killing the Jewish people after the war. There is no excuse.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had an excuse during the war, but no excuse. They were-- they were killed, Jewish people, in many villages. They were-- and this, they have no excuse. I would like to take them to trial. Why they kill so many people after the war? We're going to change the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. That's what I wanted to say because--
